questions.stem: An infant has sudden episodes of crying with knees drawn to the chest and passes a stool that looks like currant jelly. Which action should the nurse anticipate first?

options.text: Prepare the child for urgent evaluation and possible air enema reduction.
Give an antidiarrheal and observe for improvement.
Encourage the parent to offer more formula at home.
Document the stool as a common viral finding.

questions.rationale: Colicky pain with knees drawn up and currant jelly stool strongly suggests intussusception. Prompt evaluation is needed because bowel ischemia can develop if the obstruction is not reduced. Supportive home measures do not address the underlying emergency.

distractorRationales.b: "Give an antidiarrheal and observe for improvement" misses the bedside risk. The cue "Colicky pain with knees drawn up and currant jelly stool strongly suggests intussusception" points to circulation or perfusion. Perfusion problems require rapid assessment and stabilization before comfort measures or delayed provider follow-up.
distractorRationales.c: The option can leave circulation or perfusion untreated because it moves to activity, intake, or discharge planning too early. "Prepare the child for urgent evaluation and possible air enema reduction" fits the safer response to the cue "Currant jelly stool plus episodic pain is a pattern clue for intussusception, not simple gastroenteritis".
distractorRationales.d: "Document the stool as a common viral finding" misses the bedside risk. The cue "Currant jelly stool plus episodic pain is a pattern clue for intussusception, not simple gastroenteritis" points to circulation or perfusion. Perfusion problems require rapid assessment and stabilization before comfort measures or delayed provider follow-up.

questions.takeaway: Currant jelly stool plus episodic pain is a pattern clue for intussusception, not simple gastroenteritis.

questions.stem: A client who recently started haloperidol develops severe muscle rigidity, a temperature of 104 F (40 C), and fluctuating blood pressure. Which action should the nurse take first?

options.text: Hold the antipsychotic and notify the provider about possible neuroleptic malignant syndrome.
Give the next dose early to stabilize behavior.
Apply extra blankets to reduce shivering.
Wait to see if the symptoms improve after sleep.

questions.rationale: Hyperthermia, rigidity, and autonomic instability after antipsychotic therapy are classic clues for neuroleptic malignant syndrome. The triggering drug should be stopped and emergency evaluation begun immediately. Delayed recognition can lead to rhabdomyolysis, renal injury, and death.

distractorRationales.b: The safer response addresses medication toxicity; "Give the next dose early to stabilize behavior" moves care toward routine care before medication-safety intervention.
distractorRationales.c: The safer response addresses medication toxicity; "Apply extra blankets to reduce shivering" moves care toward routine care before medication-safety intervention.
distractorRationales.d: The cue "Hyperthermia, rigidity" suggests medication toxicity, so "Wait to see if the symptoms improve after sleep" would not correct the active problem. Medication safety depends on checking contraindications, labs, and adverse effects before giving or continuing the drug.

questions.takeaway: Rigidity plus high fever after an antipsychotic is a stop-the-drug emergency pattern.

visualRationale.title: neuroleptic_malignant_syndrome
visualRationale.caption: Rigidity plus high fever after an antipsychotic is a stop-the-drug emergency pattern.
visualRationale.conclusion: Rigidity plus high fever after an antipsychotic is a stop-the-drug emergency pattern.

questions.stem: A newborn of a client with gestational diabetes is jittery, weakly feeding, and has a bedside glucose of 32 mg/dL. Which action should the nurse take first?

options.text: Follow the newborn hypoglycemia protocol and feed or treat immediately.
Reassure the parent that jitteriness is normal for all newborns.
Delay intervention until the next scheduled assessment.
Place the newborn in direct sunlight to stimulate activity.

questions.rationale: Jitteriness and poor feeding with a very low glucose level are urgent clues for neonatal hypoglycemia. Early treatment prevents seizures and neurologic injury. Waiting or dismissing the signs can delay needed stabilization.

distractorRationales.b: The cue "has a bedside glucose of 32 mg/dL" suggests unstable glucose physiology, so "Reassure the parent that jitteriness is normal for all newborns" would not correct the active problem. Glucose instability can progress quickly, so medication timing and metabolic rescue come before routine follow-up.
distractorRationales.c: The safer response addresses unstable glucose physiology; "Delay intervention until the next scheduled assessment" moves care toward routine observation before glucose rescue or metabolic stabilization.
distractorRationales.d: "Place the newborn in direct sunlight to stimulate activity" answers a lower-acuity part of the scenario. The cue "Jitteriness and poor feeding with a very low glucose level are urgent clues for neonatal hypoglycemia" raises concern for unstable glucose physiology, so glucose rescue or metabolic stabilization is needed before routine care.

questions.takeaway: Newborn jitteriness is not just a detail; paired with low glucose it is the clue that should trigger immediate treatment.

visualRationale.title: newborn_hypoglycemia_jitteriness
visualRationale.caption: Newborn jitteriness is not just a detail; paired with low glucose it is the clue that should trigger immediate treatment.
visualRationale.conclusion: Newborn jitteriness is not just a detail; paired with low glucose it is the clue that should trigger immediate treatment.

questions.stem: A pregnant client at 35 weeks has sudden abdominal pain, dark vaginal bleeding, and a firm tender uterus. Which action is the priority?

options.text: Apply external fetal monitoring and notify the provider immediately.
Perform a vaginal examination to check cervical dilation.
Encourage the client to ambulate to ease discomfort.
Document the findings as expected Braxton Hicks contractions.

questions.rationale: Painful bleeding with a rigid, tender uterus suggests placental abruption. The nurse should monitor maternal-fetal status and escalate immediately because abruptions can rapidly compromise perfusion. Vaginal examination and delay can worsen an already unstable situation.

distractorRationales.b: The safer response addresses bleeding or anticoagulation injury; "Perform a vaginal examination to check cervical dilation" moves care toward routine care before bleeding-risk intervention.
distractorRationales.c: The option can leave bleeding or anticoagulation injury untreated because it moves to activity, intake, or discharge planning too early. "Apply external fetal monitoring and notify the provider immediately" fits the safer response to the cue "A pregnant client at 35 weeks has sudden abdominal pain, dark vaginal bleeding".
distractorRationales.d: This distractor shifts care toward false reassurance instead of bleeding-risk intervention. The stem cue "A pregnant client at 35 weeks has sudden abdominal pain, dark vaginal bleeding" makes bleeding or anticoagulation injury the active safety concern.

questions.takeaway: Pain plus dark bleeding and uterine tenderness is the clue cluster for placental abruption, not previa.

questions.stem: A client 2 days after surgery suddenly becomes short of breath, anxious, and tachycardic, with oxygen saturation 84% on room air. Which action should the nurse take first?

options.text: Apply oxygen and notify the provider about suspected pulmonary embolism.
Encourage the client to use the incentive spirometer and rest.
Document postoperative anxiety and reassess later.
Give oral fluids and wait for the next vital signs check.

questions.rationale: Sudden dyspnea, hypoxemia, tachycardia, and anxiety after surgery suggest pulmonary embolism until proven otherwise. Immediate oxygenation support and escalation are the priorities. Reassurance alone would miss a time-sensitive life threat.

distractorRationales.b: The cue "Sudden postoperative hypoxia and tachycardia should trigger pulmonary embolism thinking right away" suggests oxygenation or ventilation, so "Encourage the client to use the incentive spirometer and rest" would not correct the active problem. Airway assessment, positioning, oxygen support, or rapid escalation protects ventilation before teaching or documentation.
distractorRationales.c: The safer response addresses oxygenation or ventilation; "Document postoperative anxiety and reassess later" moves care toward routine observation before airway and breathing intervention.
distractorRationales.d: The option can leave oxygenation or ventilation untreated because it treats active deterioration as something safe to watch. "Apply oxygen and notify the provider about suspected pulmonary embolism" fits the safer response to the cue "anxiety after surgery suggest pulmonary embolism until proven otherwise".

questions.takeaway: Sudden postoperative hypoxia and tachycardia should trigger pulmonary embolism thinking right away.

questions.stem: A 5-week-old infant has forceful projectile vomiting after feedings and remains hungry afterward. Which finding would the nurse expect?

options.text: A palpable olive-shaped mass in the upper abdomen.
Bilious green emesis with abdominal distention.
Bloody diarrhea with lethargy.
A wet cough that worsens while feeding.

questions.rationale: Projectile nonbilious vomiting with persistent hunger is a classic clue pattern for pyloric stenosis. An olive-shaped mass may be palpable. The other findings point toward different gastrointestinal or feeding problems.

distractorRationales.b: This distractor shifts care toward premature attribution instead of bedside safety action. The stem cue "Projectile vomiting plus ongoing hunger should make pyloric stenosis one of the first patterns the student recognizes" makes pediatric or newborn instability the active safety concern.
distractorRationales.c: The option can leave pediatric or newborn instability untreated because it commits to a competing cause before checking the dangerous pattern. "A palpable olive-shaped mass in the upper abdomen" fits the safer response to the cue "Projectile vomiting plus ongoing hunger should make pyloric stenosis one of the first patterns the student recognizes".
distractorRationales.d: The safer response addresses pediatric or newborn instability; "A wet cough that worsens while feeding" moves care toward ambulation, intake, or discharge teaching before bedside safety action.

questions.takeaway: Projectile vomiting plus ongoing hunger should make pyloric stenosis one of the first patterns the student recognizes.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the current ChapAI NCLEX draft library with recent-family avoidance and curated-seed preference.

questions.stem: A client taking sertraline started a new migraine medication and now has agitation, diarrhea, fever, and hyperreflexia. Which action should the nurse take first?

options.text: Hold serotonergic medications and notify the provider about possible serotonin syndrome.
Give the next antidepressant dose to prevent withdrawal.
Encourage caffeine to improve alertness.
Document the symptoms as expected anxiety.

questions.rationale: Agitation, hyperreflexia, fever, and diarrhea after interacting serotonergic drugs suggest serotonin syndrome. The nurse should stop further serotonergic exposure and escalate care immediately. Delayed recognition can lead to worsening instability.

distractorRationales.b: The option can leave medication toxicity untreated because it commits to a competing cause before checking the dangerous pattern. "Hold serotonergic medications and notify the provider about possible serotonin syndrome" fits the safer response to the cue "Hyperreflexia and fever after serotonergic drug overlap is a clue pattern that should trigger serotonin syndrome thinking fast".
distractorRationales.c: The safer response addresses medication toxicity; "Encourage caffeine to improve alertness" moves care toward routine care before medication-safety intervention.
distractorRationales.d: "Document the symptoms as expected anxiety" misses the bedside risk. The cue "diarrhea after interacting serotonergic drugs suggest serotonin syndrome" points to medication toxicity. Medication safety depends on checking contraindications, labs, and adverse effects before giving or continuing the drug.

questions.takeaway: Hyperreflexia and fever after serotonergic drug overlap is a clue pattern that should trigger serotonin syndrome thinking fast.

questions.stem: A client with sodium 118 mEq/L becomes confused and then has a seizure. Which action should the nurse anticipate first?

options.text: Prepare for hypertonic saline administration and seizure precautions.
Give free water to replace the sodium loss.
Encourage the client to ambulate to reduce confusion.
Delay intervention until another sodium level is drawn.

questions.rationale: Severe symptomatic hyponatremia with neurologic signs is an emergency. The nurse should anticipate controlled correction and seizure management rather than free water or delay. The key clue is not just the lab value, but the combination of low sodium plus acute neurologic deterioration.

distractorRationales.b: "Give free water to replace the sodium loss" moves to activity, intake, or discharge planning too early. The cue "the combination of low sodium plus acute neurologic deterioration" raises concern for neurologic deterioration, so neurologic safety assessment is needed before ambulation, intake, or discharge teaching.
distractorRationales.c: The option can leave neurologic deterioration untreated because it moves to activity, intake, or discharge planning too early. "Prepare for hypertonic saline administration and seizure precautions" fits the safer response to the cue "A client with sodium 118 mEq/L becomes confused and then has a seizure".
distractorRationales.d: "Delay intervention until another sodium level is drawn" misses the bedside risk. The cue "the combination of low sodium plus acute neurologic deterioration" points to neurologic deterioration. Neurologic change needs prompt assessment and safety measures before reassurance, discharge, or routine reassessment.

questions.takeaway: When low sodium is paired with confusion or seizure, the student should move immediately toward emergency correction thinking.

questions.stem: A client has been actively seizing for more than 5 minutes despite airway positioning and oxygen. Which medication should the nurse expect to administer first?

options.text: An IV benzodiazepine such as lorazepam.
An oral antiepileptic when the seizure stops.
A stimulant to improve alertness.
No medication until a head CT is completed.

questions.rationale: Status epilepticus requires rapid medication treatment after immediate airway and safety support. IV benzodiazepines are first-line for stopping active seizures. Waiting for imaging or oral therapy delays the intervention that actually stops the seizure.

distractorRationales.b: The cue "A client has been actively seizing for more than 5 minutes despite airway positioning and oxygen" suggests oxygenation or ventilation, so "An oral antiepileptic when the seizure stops" would not correct the active problem. Airway assessment, positioning, oxygen support, or rapid escalation protects ventilation before teaching or documentation.
distractorRationales.c: "A stimulant to improve alertness" misses the bedside risk. The cue "Status epilepticus requires rapid medication treatment after immediate airway and safety support" points to oxygenation or ventilation. Airway assessment, positioning, oxygen support, or rapid escalation protects ventilation before teaching or documentation.
distractorRationales.d: The cue "A client has been actively seizing for more than 5 minutes despite airway positioning and oxygen" suggests oxygenation or ventilation, so "No medication until a head CT is completed" would not correct the active problem. Airway assessment, positioning, oxygen support, or rapid escalation protects ventilation before teaching or documentation.

questions.takeaway: Past the 5-minute mark, seizure care shifts from watchful support to benzodiazepine-first emergency treatment.

questions.stem: A laboring client receives an epidural and 5 minutes later becomes pale and dizzy. Blood pressure is 78/42 mmHg and the fetal heart rate shows recurrent late decelerations. Which action should the nurse take first?

options.text: Reposition the client and increase the IV fluid rate.
Encourage the client to push with the next contraction.
Lower the head of the bed and leave the client flat.
Document an expected epidural response and reassess later.

questions.rationale: Maternal hypotension after epidural analgesia can reduce uteroplacental perfusion and trigger late decelerations. The nurse should reposition the client and support circulating volume immediately. Delaying action increases fetal hypoxia risk.

distractorRationales.b: The safer response addresses circulation or perfusion; "Encourage the client to push with the next contraction" moves care toward diagnostic reassurance before circulation and perfusion support.
distractorRationales.c: "Lower the head of the bed and leave the client flat" answers a lower-acuity part of the scenario. The cue "Delaying action increases fetal hypoxia risk" raises concern for circulation or perfusion, so circulation and perfusion support is needed before routine care.
distractorRationales.d: The cue "Delaying action increases fetal hypoxia risk" suggests circulation or perfusion, so "Document an expected epidural response and reassess later" would not correct the active problem. Perfusion problems require rapid assessment and stabilization before comfort measures or delayed provider follow-up.

questions.takeaway: Maternal hypotension after epidural should be corrected quickly to restore fetal perfusion.

questions.stem: A client with acute kidney injury has a potassium level of 6.8 mEq/L and peaked T waves on the cardiac monitor. Which action is the priority?

options.text: Prepare to administer IV calcium gluconate.
Encourage the client to increase oral fluids.
Place the client on a potassium-restricted diet.
Repeat the potassium level in 4 hours.

questions.rationale: Hyperkalemia with ECG changes places the client at immediate risk for lethal dysrhythmias. IV calcium gluconate stabilizes the cardiac membrane while other therapies work to lower serum potassium. Dietary teaching and delayed reassessment do not address the urgent electrical instability.

distractorRationales.b: The option can leave circulation or perfusion untreated because it moves to activity, intake, or discharge planning too early. "Prepare to administer IV calcium gluconate" fits the safer response to the cue "IV calcium gluconate stabilizes the cardiac membrane while other therapies work to lower serum potassium".
distractorRationales.c: The safer response addresses circulation or perfusion; "Place the client on a potassium-restricted diet" moves care toward routine care before circulation and perfusion support.
distractorRationales.d: This distractor shifts care toward premature activity instead of circulation and perfusion support. The stem cue "Hyperkalemia plus peaked T waves is a cardiac emergency: stabilize the myocardium first" makes circulation or perfusion the active safety concern.

questions.takeaway: Hyperkalemia plus peaked T waves is a cardiac emergency: stabilize the myocardium first.

questions.stem: A client taking lithium for bipolar disorder has vomiting, coarse hand tremors, and increasing confusion after several days of diarrhea. Which action should the nurse take first?

options.text: Hold the lithium and notify the provider.
Give the lithium with crackers to reduce stomach upset.
Teach the client to double the next dose.
Encourage the client to restrict sodium intake further.

questions.rationale: Vomiting, worsening tremor, and confusion after fluid loss suggest lithium toxicity. The nurse should stop further doses and notify the provider for prompt evaluation. Dehydration and sodium loss can raise lithium levels, so more restriction or additional dosing would be unsafe.

distractorRationales.b: The cue "Dehydration and sodium loss can raise lithium levels, so more restriction or additional dosing would be unsafe" suggests medication toxicity, so "Give the lithium with crackers to reduce stomach upset" would not correct the active problem. Medication safety depends on checking contraindications, labs, and adverse effects before giving or continuing the drug.
distractorRationales.c: "Teach the client to double the next dose" misses the bedside risk. The cue "GI loss plus confusion and coarse tremor on lithium should be treated as toxicity until proven otherwise" points to medication toxicity. Medication safety depends on checking contraindications, labs, and adverse effects before giving or continuing the drug.
distractorRationales.d: The cue "Dehydration and sodium loss can raise lithium levels, so more restriction or additional dosing would be unsafe" suggests medication toxicity, so "Encourage the client to restrict sodium intake further" would not correct the active problem. Medication safety depends on checking contraindications, labs, and adverse effects before giving or continuing the drug.

questions.takeaway: GI loss plus confusion and coarse tremor on lithium should be treated as toxicity until proven otherwise.
